case_name: Wilcox v. Advocate Condell Medical Center

summary.brief: Wilcox v. Advocate Condell Medical Center. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: Scott Wilcox suffered an injury in a skiing accident at age 26 that rendered him paralyzed from the armpits down.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A pump was implanted into his abdomen that administered the antispasmodic drug baclofen into the intrathecal space of his spinal canal via a catheter.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In the late afternoon of Friday, July 28, 2017, Scott saw Dr. Richard Senno, the physical medicine and rehabilitation physician who treated his muscle spasticity and managed his baclofen pump.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Senno wanted him to be admitted to a hospital so that he could be monitored for symptoms of baclofen withdrawal until his pump could be surgically replaced.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Scott drove with his caretaker to Advocate Condell, and he was admitted that evening.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Scott was being admitted due to the malfunction of his baclofen pump and concern of the potential for baclofen withdrawal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Scott was assigned a hospitalist, Dr. Eric Chuang, and a pain management physician, Dr. Yulia Kin-Kartsimas.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Jim testified that, when they saw Scott early on Saturday afternoon, he appeared normal.
evidence_index.speaker: Witness statement

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a verdict in favor of the plaintiff and against Advocate on both the institutional negligence and vicarious liability claims.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The judgment of the trial court is affirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Malfunctioning baclofen pump with risk of withdrawal
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Scott Wilcox, who is paralyzed from the armpits down due to a past skiing accident, presented with a malfunctioning baclofen pump. The pump alarm had sounded, indicating it was not working, and there was concern for baclofen withdrawal.

focused_exam.neurological: Slight spasms noted, otherwise appeared well

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Baclofen pump malfunction with risk of withdrawal

requestables.name: Neurology Consult
requestables.description: Consultation to manage spasticity and monitor for withdrawal symptoms

reveal.result_summary: Neurologist noted increased muscle spasms, no mental status deficits

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to hospital with malfunctioning baclofen pump

timeline_phases.name: Monitoring and Assessment
timeline_phases.description: Patient monitored for withdrawal symptoms over the weekend

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient admitted with malfunctioning baclofen pump
decision_points.prompt: What should be the immediate management plan?

options.description: Schedule immediate surgery for pump replacement
options.clinical_reasoning: Immediate replacement needed to prevent withdrawal

options.description: Monitor patient and schedule surgery for next week
options.clinical_reasoning: Delay increases risk of withdrawal complications

actual_action_defendant.description: Surgery was scheduled for Monday or Tuesday, not immediately

expected_action_court.description: Immediate surgery should have been scheduled
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated urgency due to withdrawal risk

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in surgery led to withdrawal symptoms and complications
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Immediate surgical intervention to replace pump
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care for managing baclofen withdrawal risk

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to provide continuous monitoring
Causation established - inadequate monitoring led to withdrawal symptoms

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Continuous monitoring would have detected withdrawal symptoms earlier

factual_timeline.event: Patient admitted to hospital with malfunctioning baclofen pump
Patient's condition worsens with withdrawal symptoms
Patient passed away after being on life support

treatments.outcome: No complications with surgery, but patient did not recover

complications.complication: Multiorgan failure due to baclofen withdrawal

allegations.allegation: Failure to promptly replace malfunctioning baclofen pump

court_findings.finding: Institutional negligence due to systems failure and communication breakdown
court_findings.legal_significance: Breach of duty leading to patient's death

court_findings.finding: Institutional negligence due to communication failures
